disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring reduced overall fire risk from lower DSC ΔH alone
Treating anode-electrolyte ΔH as total system energy proxy

solver_view.stem: For Li-ion batteries cycled across charge/discharge ambient temperatures (CTE) from 30°C to 60°C, the LEL of thermal runaway gas reaches its minimum at 40°C CTE and increases for temperatures above 40°C. DMC has a boiling point of 90.1°C and decomposes only above 200°C. Which of the following correctly explain why LEL increases as CTE rises from 40°C to 60°C?
options: A. Above 50°C CTE, cathode material activity consumes more DMC than is generated by increased internal gas production, reducing DMC content and raising LEL.
B. Above 40°C CTE, higher ambient temperature causes DMC to decompose before thermal runaway, directly removing it from the gas phase.
C. Increasing CTE from 40°C to 60°C raises the exothermic ΔH of anode-electrolyte reactions, consuming more electrolyte and leaving less DMC to vaporize.
D. At CTE above 40°C, SEI thickening accelerates, producing more CO2 which raises the LEL of the gas mixture by diluting combustible fractions.
E. The LEL increase from 40°C to 60°C CTE is driven by a reduction in combustible hydrocarbon proportions relative to non-combustible gases in the thermal runaway vent.
F. Because DMC boiling point is 90.1°C, elevated CTE above 50°C causes DMC to remain liquid during cycling, preventing its entry into thermal runaway gas entirely.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states above 50°C, cathode material activity effect on DMC exceeds internal gas production, lowering DMC content.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: DMC decomposes only above 200°C; ambient CTE of 50–60°C cannot cause DMC decomposition during cycling.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Higher CTE increases anode-electrolyte ΔH, consuming more electrolyte and reducing vaporized DMC in thermal runaway gas.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: SEI thickening is linked to overcharge voltage, not increasing CTE; attributing it to temperature is a mechanism substitution.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Plausible restatement but not independently supported; the mechanism must trace through DMC reduction specifically.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: CTE of 50–60°C is below DMC boiling point of 90.1°C; this reasoning misapplies the boiling point threshold.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanism linking CTE above 40°C to LEL increase via DMC consumption pathways
analysis.primary_boundary: DMC decomposition threshold is >200°C; CTE range 40–60°C affects DMC via electrolyte consumption, not direct thermal decomposition
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying DMC decomposition threshold to ambient CTE conditions
Attributing SEI thickening to temperature rather than overcharge voltage

solver_view.prompt: A Li-ion battery is overcharged to a cutoff voltage of 4.4 V and cycled at a charge/discharge ambient temperature of 40°C. Explain the complete causal chain by which these conditions produce a lower LEL in thermal runaway gas compared to a normally charged (4.2 V) battery cycled at 30°C. Your answer must address: (1) the role of lithium precipitation at the negative electrode, (2) the role of SEI membrane thickening, (3) why CO increase does not dominate the LEL decrease, and (4) why 40°C CTE produces a lower LEL than either 30°C or temperatures above 40°C.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Overcharging to 4.4 V causes lithium precipitation at the negative electrode. Precipitated lithium reacts with electrolyte DMC, but the key effect is that more DMC vaporizes into the thermal runaway gas because less electrolyte is consumed by anode reactions (lower DSC ΔH at 4.4 V). Simultaneously, SEI thickening from overcharge cycling causes incomplete oxidation reactions during thermal runaway, reducing CO2 production and increasing CO content. However, CO has a LEL of 12%, far above combustible hydrocarbons (1.5–5%), so increased CO does not lower the mixture LEL significantly. The LEL decrease is instead driven by reduced CO2 (a non-combustible diluent) and increased vaporized DMC and other combustible hydrocarbons. At 40°C CTE, anode-electrolyte ΔH is lower than at higher temperatures, leaving more unreacted DMC to vaporize. Above 50°C, cathode material activity consumes DMC faster than internal gas production generates it, raising DMC content and LEL. Below 40°C (e.g., 30°C), less lithium precipitation and less DMC vaporization occur. Thus 40°C CTE combined with 4.4 V overcharge maximizes vaporized DMC and minimizes LEL.
must_include: lithium precipitation at negative electrode increases vaporized DMC
SEI thickening causes incomplete CO2 production and CO increase
CO LEL is 12% so CO increase does not dominate LEL decrease
LEL decrease driven by reduced CO2 and increased combustible hydrocarbon proportion
40°C CTE yields lowest LEL; above 50°C cathode activity reduces DMC content raising LEL
higher CTE increases anode-electrolyte ΔH consuming more electrolyte reducing vaporized DMC
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming CO increase is the primary driver of LEL decrease
Stating DMC decomposes at CTE of 40–60°C (decomposition requires >200°C)
Generalizing LEL decrease beyond the tested overcharge gradient of up to 2% total change
Inferring that higher overcharge always produces proportionally greater fire hazard from LEL alone

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Full multi-step causal chain connecting overcharge voltage and CTE to LEL via DMC vaporization, SEI thickening, CO2 reduction, and CO LEL hierarchy
analysis.primary_boundary: Overall LEL change does not exceed 2%; CO LEL of 12% places it outside the dominant LEL-lowering mechanism; DMC decomposition threshold is >200°C not reachable by CTE alone
intended_error_modes: causal_overclaim (treating lower DSC ΔH as reduced total hazard)
regime_transfer_error (assuming LEL trend above 40°C continues below 40°C)

solver_view.stem: A safety engineer compares LFP and NMC 18650 cells at 100% SOC undergoing thermal runaway in an enclosed space. She evaluates flammability risk using lower flammability limit (LFL), total gas volume produced, and likelihood of vapour cloud formation. Which of the following statements are correct regarding LFP relative to NMC under these conditions?
options: A. LFP off-gas has a lower LFL than NMC off-gas, attributed to higher H2 fractions and lower CO and CO2 fractions.
B. LFP cells produce more total off-gas volume per unit capacity than NMC cells at 100% SOC, increasing vapour cloud risk.
C. LFP cells are more likely to emit vent gas without combustion due to lower thermal runaway temperatures, increasing vapour cloud accumulation risk.
D. LFP off-gas breaches its LFL in a smaller volume than NMC off-gas when both are released in an inert atmosphere.
E. NMC cells present greater vapour cloud explosion risk than LFP because NMC produces higher CO fractions that lower the mixture LFL.
F. LFP and NMC off-gases have equivalent LFL values because H2 dominates both mixtures at 100% SOC.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: LFP LFL is 6.2% vs NMC 7.9–9.2%, driven by higher H2 and lower CO/CO2 fractions in LFP off-gas.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: At 100% SOC, LFP produces 0.4–1.4 L/Ah versus NMC up to 21 L/Ah; LFP volume is lower, not higher.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lower LFP TR temperature means vent gas is more likely emitted without combustion, raising vapour cloud accumulation risk.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: LFP breaches LFL in a volume 18% smaller than NMC in inert atmosphere, combining lower LFL and gas volume data.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: CO has a large LFL, so higher CO in NMC raises the mixture LFL rather than lowering it; LFP has greater vapour cloud risk.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: LFL values differ (LFP 6.2%, NMC 7.9–9.2%) due to differing CO, CO2, and H2 fractions; they are not equivalent.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-factor flammability risk comparison between LFP and NMC: LFL value, gas volume, combustion likelihood, and vapour cloud risk
analysis.primary_boundary: Conditions fixed at 100% SOC, inert atmosphere for volume comparison; LFL attribution requires full gas composition reasoning
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalising LFP higher vapour cloud risk to all SOC levels
Assuming higher gas volume always implies higher flammability risk without LFL consideration

solver_view.stem: An incident investigator assesses toxic gas hazard from a 0.01 kWh LFP battery and a 0.01 kWh NMC battery undergoing thermal runaway, each evaluated at both 0% and 100% SOC. She considers HF and CO as the primary toxic components. Which of the following statements about toxicity trends are INCORRECT?
options: A. At 0% SOC, LFP produces more toxic contaminated volume than NMC because LFP emits substantially more HF per unit capacity.
B. As SOC increases from 0% to 100%, NMC contaminated volume more than doubles, driven primarily by rising CO emissions.
C. As SOC increases from 0% to 100%, LFP contaminated volume increases because HF emissions rise with SOC.
D. At 100% SOC, NMC emits approximately 10 times more CO per unit capacity than LFP, making NMC more acutely toxic via CO at that state.
E. LFP HF emissions are lower than NMC at all SOC levels when normalised to capacity.
F. The dominant driver of LFP toxicity reduction from 0% to 100% SOC is decreasing HF output linked to higher thermal runaway temperatures.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At 0% SOC, LFP contaminated volumes (CO 355 m³, HF 695 m³) far exceed NMC, confirming greater toxicity at low SOC.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: NMC CO rises from ~10 g/kWh at 25% SOC to 172 g/kWh at 100% SOC, more than doubling contaminated volume.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: LFP HF emissions decrease with SOC (100 g/kWh at 0% to 50 g/kWh at 100%), so contaminated volume halves, not increases.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: NMC emits 172 g/kWh CO vs LFP 19 g/kWh at 100% SOC, approximately a 9–10× difference confirming CO-driven NMC toxicity.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: LFP emits 50–100 g/kWh HF versus NMC <10 g/kWh; LFP HF is over ten times higher than NMC, not lower.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: LFP HF downward trend with SOC is attributed to higher TR temperatures at higher SOC, consistent with evidence.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Directional toxicity trends with SOC for LFP and NMC, distinguishing HF and CO contributions and their opposing SOC dependencies
analysis.primary_boundary: Stem asks for INCORRECT statements; polarity inversion required. LFP HF trend is downward with SOC; LFP HF is higher than NMC per capacity.
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming LFP is always safer than NMC across all metrics
Extending NMC CO trend to HF or to LFP chemistry

solver_view.stem: A pack designer is selecting cell format and chemistry for a stationary storage system and must minimise off-gas volume per unit energy. At 100% SOC, she compares cylindrical versus prismatic cell formats and LFP versus NMC chemistry. She also considers whether scaling capacity linearly from a single cell to a multi-cell pack is valid for gas hazard estimation. Which statements are supported by evidence?
options: A. Cylindrical cells produce substantially less off-gas per unit energy than prismatic cells at 100% SOC in both air and inert atmospheres.
B. NMC chemistry produces more off-gas per unit energy than LFP at 100% SOC, so combining NMC with cylindrical format does not guarantee lower gas output than LFP prismatic.
C. Linear scaling of gas production from one cell to a pack of equivalent capacity is always valid because gas generation is a capacity-proportional electrochemical process.
D. At 100% SOC, a group of 10 cells produces 25% more gas per unit capacity than one large cell of similar total capacity, but this difference disappears at lower SOC.
E. Overcharge abuse generates more gas than over-temperature or nail penetration, so format selection is irrelevant for overcharge scenarios.
F. Aged cells generate more gas per unit capacity than new cells because ageing increases electrode reactivity.
G. The greatest per-unit increase in gas emissions with rising SOC occurs between 50% and 100% SOC, regardless of chemistry.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Cylindrical median ~156–157 L/kWh versus prismatic ~573–598 L/kWh in both atmospheres; difference is substantial.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: NMC median 519 L/kWh vs LFP 126 L/kWh; chemistry effect can outweigh format, so NMC cylindrical vs LFP prismatic requires both factors.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: HRR does not scale linearly; caution is advised for all battery hazards when scaling with capacity, not just HRR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Gas production is 25% greater for 10 cells vs one large cell at 100% SOC; difference is negligible at 75% and 50% SOC.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Overcharge generating more gas does not make format irrelevant; format still affects absolute gas volume and enclosure risk.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Aged cells show less gas generation because more electrolyte is consumed; ageing reduces, not increases, gas output.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Greatest increase is typically over 50–100% SOC, but evidence does not confirm this is chemistry-independent; 'regardless of chemistry' is an unsupported universal claim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-factor off-gas minimisation: format effect, chemistry effect, scaling validity, and SOC-dependent emission trends
analysis.primary_boundary: Scaling claim requires non-linear caveat; 25% multi-cell excess is SOC-conditional (only at 100% SOC); aged cell direction is counterintuitive
disallowed_extrapolations: Universal linear scaling of any battery hazard with capacity
Extending 25% multi-cell excess to all SOC levels

solver_view.prompt: A fire safety engineer is designing ventilation and explosion prevention protocols for a battery storage room containing both LFP and NMC cells. She notes that LFP cells produce less total off-gas per unit capacity than NMC at 100% SOC, and concludes that LFP therefore poses a lower vapour cloud explosion risk than NMC. Identify the flaws in her reasoning by explaining (1) why lower gas volume alone does not determine vapour cloud explosion risk, (2) what property of LFP off-gas composition makes it more hazardous per unit volume of gas released, and (3) what thermal runaway characteristic of LFP further elevates vapour cloud risk relative to NMC.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The engineer's conclusion is flawed on three grounds. First, explosion risk depends on both gas volume and the lower flammability limit (LFL): LFP off-gas has a lower LFL (6.2%) than NMC off-gas (7.9–9.2%), meaning LFP gas reaches an explosive concentration in a smaller volume. Combining lower LFL with gas volume data, LFP off-gas breaches its LFL in a volume 18% smaller than NMC, partially offsetting the gas volume advantage. Second, the lower LFL of LFP is driven by its higher H2 fraction and lower CO and CO2 fractions relative to NMC; CO has a high individual LFL and CO2 dilutes the mixture, so NMC's richer CO/CO2 composition raises its mixture LFL. Third, LFP cells have lower thermal runaway temperatures than NMC, making it more likely that vent gas is released without ignition, allowing unburned flammable vapour to accumulate as a vapour cloud rather than combust at the cell, directly elevating vapour cloud explosion risk.
must_include: LFP LFL (6.2%) is lower than NMC LFL (7.9–9.2%)
LFP breaches LFL in a volume 18% smaller than NMC in inert atmosphere
Higher H2 and lower CO/CO2 fractions explain LFP lower LFL
Lower LFP thermal runaway temperature increases probability of vent gas emission without combustion
Unburned vapour accumulation increases vapour cloud explosion risk for LFP
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming LFP is always more dangerous than NMC across all hazard dimensions
Extending the 18% smaller breach volume finding to non-inert or fire conditions
Asserting LFP has lower total toxicity than NMC without specifying the metric and SOC

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-factor vapour cloud explosion risk: interaction of gas volume, LFL value, gas composition drivers of LFL, and thermal runaway temperature effect on ignition probability
analysis.primary_boundary: 18% smaller breach volume applies to inert atmosphere median conditions only; LFL attribution requires correct identification of H2, CO, and CO2 roles
intended_error_modes: mechanism substitution confusing CO role in LFL
boundary overreach generalising inert-atmosphere finding to all conditions
